attributes.stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted with a two day history of left iliac fossa pain associated with diarrhoea. He is normally in good health and does not take any medications. On examination he has a temperature of 38C and there is a tender mass in his left iliac fossa. A CT scan confirms the diagnosis of diverticulitis. He is started on broad spectrum antibiotics and a number of investigations performed. One of these shows a serum calcium of 3.0mmol/L (2.1-2.6).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for this finding?
attributes.explanation:
This 55-year-old man was admitted for left-iliac fossa pain associated with diarrhea, likely due to acute diverticulitis. Otherwise, he is in good health. Lab tests reveal an otherwise relatively asymptomatic individual with hypercalcemia. Whilst any of the listed options could be associated with hypercalcemia the detection of asymptomatic hypercalcemia is most in keeping with the diagnosis of primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT).

The initial step in the evaluation of hypercalcemia is to confirm this diagnosis with a repeat measurement and correct for albumin. Of the causes of hypercalcemia, PHPT and malignancy comprise the most common aetiologies, accounting for more than 90% of cases. Possible clues suggesting a diagnosis come in the form of systemic symptoms and the degree of hypercalcemia. Compared with PHPT hypercalcemia of malignancy can manifest with constitutional signs (weight loss, tiredness, fever, etc.) and typically results in a greater degree of elevation in serum concentrations of calcium (PHPT is borderline or mildly elevated) and a quick rise in serum levels which results in symptomatic manifestations. The classic symptoms of PHPT can be recalled using the memory aid “bones (bone disease/pain), stones (nephrolithiasis), abdominal moans (constipation), and psychic groans (neurobehavioral symptoms)” which are the result of increased calcium in the setting of elevated PTH. In Western countries, most cases of PHPT remain asymptomatic and are identified inadvertently by laboratory testing. In countries where such screening is not routine, PHPT is usually diagnosed on the basis of renal stone disease or target organ involvement.

Other options:

  • Sarcoidosis: Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease and a known cause of hypercalcemia to elevated 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D levels due to activation of extrarenal 1-alpha-hydroxylase within epithelioid histocytes. Sarcoidosis typically affects young adults and involving the lungs (in 90% of cases) presenting with pulmonary opacification or hilar adenopathy on CXR or skin, eye, and joint involvement – none of which are seen here.

  • Multiple myeloma: In patients with MM, hypercalcemia is due to an interplay of cytokines and growth factors. The median age of diagnosis is 65-74 and diagnosis should be suspected when hypercalcemia is identified in the setting of renal failure, unexplained anemia, bone pain with lytic lesions (on imaging). These features are sometimes referred to with the “CRAB criteria.” This patient presents with a myriad of symptoms are which are inconsistent with MM making another diagnosis more likely.

  • Carcinoma of the bronchus: Typical signs of lung cancer include cough, hemoptysis, and dyspnoea. These signs usually indicate advanced disease. While this patient has no apparent risk factors for lung cancer, the diagnosis should always be suspected in a current or former smoker. Less common manifestations of lung cancer include paraneoplastic syndromes which can include SIADH and hypercalcemia of malignancy (PHTrP release from squamous cell carcinomas). While this is an important diagnosis to consider in the setting of hypercalcemia, the clinical scenario is better explained by another diagnosis. 

  • Metastatic renal cell carcinoma: Hypercalcemia occurs in up to 15% of patients with metastatic RCC and can be attributed to lytic bone lesions, production of PTHrP, and interplay of various cytokines. Most patients with RCCs are asymptomatic but the classic triad “flank pain, a palpable abdominal pass and haematuria” may be present. Risk factors for RCC include smoking, hypertension, obesity, parenchymal kidney disease, and occupational exposure – all of which are absent in the above scenario. Given the patient's risk profile and clinical presentation, another diagnosis is more likely.
learningPoints: PHPT is typically diagnosed by detecting elevated PTH in a patient with asymptomatic hypercalcemia coupled with low phosphate (in the absence of renal failure).
PHPT and malignancy comprise the most common aetiologies of hypercalcemia, accounting for more than 90% of cases.
references: Bilezikian JP et al. Hyperparathyroidism. Lancet 2018 Jan 13;391(10116):168-178.
Fontenot Pathology and pathogenesis of sarcoidosis [Internet]. Uptodate.com 2021 [cited 05 Oct 2021]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/pathology-and-pathogenesis-of-sarcoidosis>

Laubach, JP., Multiple myeloma: Clinical features, laboratory manifestations, and diagnosis [Internet]. Uptodate.com 2021 [cited 05 Oct 2021]. Available from: < https://www.uptodate.com/contents/multiple-myeloma-clinical-features-laboratory-manifestations-and-diagnosis>

Thomas, K., Gould, Overview of the initial evaluation, diagnosis, and staging of patients with suspected lung cancer [Internet]. Uptodate.com 2021 [cited 05 Oct 2021]. Available from: < https://www.uptodate.com/contents/overview-of-the-initial-evaluation-diagnosis-and-staging-of-patients-with-suspected-lung-cancer>
